Description: NTDS database health and integrity checks

Checks.PassMessage: NTDS database is healthy on all DCs

EvaluationRules.Message: NTDS database state indicates improper shutdown or corruption
ESE database errors detected in Application event log
NTDS database health issue detected
NTDS database health check reported failure

Checks.Help: Checks the integrity and health of the NTDS.dit Active Directory database on each DC. Database corruption can cause replication failures, login issues, and data loss.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Run 'ntdsutil' then 'files' then 'integrity' to check for corruption. 2. For semantic errors run 'ntdsutil' then 'semantic database analysis' then 'go'. 3. If corruption found, consider restoring from backup or performing metadata cleanup. 4. Ensure sufficient disk space on the NTDS volume.

Checks.PassMessage: NTDS database fragmentation is within acceptable limits
Checks.FailMessage: NTDS database fragmentation detected - consider offline defrag

EvaluationRules.Message: High database fragmentation - offline defragmentation recommended
Moderate database fragmentation detected
Database fragmentation issue detected

Checks.Help: Checks the level of index fragmentation in the NTDS.dit database. High fragmentation increases query times and can slow AD operations across the forest.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Schedule an offline defragmentation during a maintenance window. 2. Boot DC to Directory Services Restore Mode (DSRM). 3. Run: ntdsutil 'activate instance ntds' 'files' 'compact to C:\temp\ntds' quit quit. 4. Replace original ntds.dit with compacted version. 5. Delete old log files and reboot.

Checks.PassMessage: Transaction log size is within normal limits on all DCs

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical number of transaction logs accumulating - replication or backup issue
Transaction logs consuming excessive disk space
Elevated number of transaction logs accumulating
Transaction logs consuming significant disk space
Transaction log issue detected

Checks.Help: Monitors the accumulation of NTDS transaction log files (edb*.log). Large numbers of logs indicate replication has stalled or backups are not triggering log truncation.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Check replication health with 'repadmin /replsummary'. 2. Resolve any replication errors - logs cannot be truncated until all replication partners are in sync. 3. After replication is healthy, run a system state backup to trigger log truncation. 4. Verify NTDS log path has sufficient disk space.

Checks.PassMessage: NTDS database log files are within normal limits on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: NTDS database log file accumulation detected

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical number of NTDS transaction logs - replication or backup is likely broken
Elevated NTDS transaction log count - check replication and backup
NTDS database white space analysis issue detected

Checks.Help: Analyzes NTDS.dit database file size relative to transaction logs to estimate white space (reclaimed space from deleted objects). Excessive white space wastes disk and can degrade performance.
Checks.Remediation: 1. If log count is high, fix replication first (see DB-003). 2. To reclaim white space perform offline defragmentation (see DB-002). 3. Consider enabling AD Recycle Bin to reduce permanent deletions (see BACKUP-003). 4. Monitor log growth rate to detect replication issues early.

Checks.PassMessage: NTDS database size and disk capacity are within acceptable limits on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: NTDS database size or disk capacity issues detected

EvaluationRules.Message: NTDS.dit exceeds 50 GB on one or more DCs - investigate object bloat
Critical disk space shortage on NTDS volume - less than 2 GB free
NTDS.dit exceeds 10 GB on one or more DCs - monitor growth trend
Database size or disk capacity issue detected

Checks.Help: Monitors NTDS.dit file size and available disk space on the NTDS volume. A growing database with insufficient free space can cause the AD DS service to stop when the disk fills up. Typical NTDS.dit sizes: small domain <1 GB, medium domain 1-5 GB, large enterprise 10-50 GB. Databases over 10 GB should be monitored for growth rate.
Checks.Remediation: 1. If disk space is low: add storage to the NTDS volume, or move NTDS to a larger volume using: ntdsutil 'activate instance ntds' 'files' 'move db to <new path>'. 2. If database is unexpectedly large: enable AD Recycle Bin, run offline defrag to reclaim white space. 3. Identify large OUs: Get-ADObject -SearchScope Subtree -ResultSetSize $null | Group-Object ObjectClass | Sort Count -Descending. 4. Check for computer/user object bloat from provisioning tools.

Checks.PassMessage: NTDS database defragmentation is current on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: NTDS database defragmentation status issues detected

EvaluationRules.Message: NTDS offline defragmentation not performed in over 1 year on one or more DCs
Database defragmentation issue detected

Checks.Help: The NTDS.dit database accumulates internal fragmentation and white space as objects are added and deleted. Online defragmentation runs automatically every 12 hours but can only reclaim space within existing database pages. Offline defragmentation (run with ntdsutil while AD DS is stopped) compacts the database file, reclaims all white space, rebuilds indexes, and can significantly reduce database size. Microsoft recommends offline defrag when white space exceeds 20% of database size.
Checks.Remediation:
1. Offline defrag requires stopping the AD DS service on a non-critical DC. 2. On the DC: Stop-Service NTDS -Force. 3. Run ntdsutil: ntdsutil 'activate instance ntds' 'files' 'compact to C:	emp
tds' quit quit. 4. Replace the original: copy C:	emp
tds
tds.dit C:\Windows\NTDS
tds.dit. 5. Delete old log files: del C:\Windows\NTDS\*.log. 6. Start-Service NTDS. 7. The event ID 700 will be logged upon successful completion.

Checks.PassMessage: Deleted object accumulation and tombstone lifetime are within acceptable parameters
Checks.FailMessage: Deleted object accumulation or tombstone lifetime issues detected

EvaluationRules.Message: Tombstone lifetime is below 60 days - risk of replication inconsistency if DC is restored from backup
More than 500 objects deleted in the last 30 days - verify bulk deletion was intentional
More than 10,000 tombstoned objects detected - high deleted object accumulation
Deleted object accumulation issue detected

Checks.Help: When objects are deleted in AD they become tombstones (soft-deleted) for the duration of the tombstone lifetime (default 180 days) before being permanently purged. High tombstone counts can indicate accidental bulk deletions, misconfigured provisioning tools, or simply a large domain. The tombstone lifetime is critical for backup/restore: if a DC is restored from a backup older than the tombstone lifetime, it may re-introduce deleted objects causing replication conflicts (lingering objects).
Checks.Remediation: 1. Check tombstone lifetime: (Get-ADObject -Identity 'CN=Directory Service,CN=Windows NT,CN=Services,CN=Configuration,DC=...' -Properties tombstoneLifetime).tombstoneLifetime. 2. Set tombstone lifetime to 180 days if lower: Set-ADObject ... -Replace @{tombstoneLifetime=180}. 3. If bulk deletion is suspected, review recent deletions: Get-ADObject -IncludeDeletedObjects -Filter {isDeleted -eq $true} -Properties whenChanged | Sort whenChanged -Descending | Select -First 20. 4. Enable AD Recycle Bin to allow recovery: Enable-ADOptionalFeature 'Recycle Bin Feature'.

Checks.PassMessage: No critical ESE database error events detected on domain controllers
Checks.FailMessage: Critical ESE database error events detected

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical ESENT database error events detected - database integrity may be compromised
ESENT database warning events detected - monitor for escalation
ESE database event issue detected

Checks.Help: ESENT (Extensible Storage Engine) is the database engine underlying NTDS.dit. ESENT logs critical events to the Application event log when it detects database corruption, dirty shutdowns, I/O errors, or checksum mismatches. These events must be investigated immediately - a corrupt NTDS database can cause AD DS to fail to start after a reboot. Key events: 454 (recovery failed), 508 (dirty shutdown), 510/512 (corruption detected), 533 (needs repair).
Checks.Remediation: 1. For dirty shutdown (Event 508/512/533): attempt soft recovery by starting AD DS service - ESENT will replay logs automatically. 2. If startup fails after dirty shutdown: run ntdsutil 'activate instance ntds' 'files' 'recover' to perform soft recovery. 3. For corruption events (454/510): run ntdsutil 'activate instance ntds' 'files' 'integrity' to check integrity. 4. If database is corrupt: restore from last good backup, or use ntdsutil 'semantic database analysis' to identify and fix issues. 5. After any fix, run dcdiag /test:advertising on all DCs to verify AD DS is fully functional.
